Action: Query CRM (e.g. Salesforce) for the account record using the account ID. Retrieve account metadata including ARR, contract dates, tier, key contacts, and recent activity log.
Output: Account metadata (ARR, contract dates, tier, key contacts, recent activity log)
Decision Logic Detail: None — data retrieval. Query CRM using account ID to retrieve a single structured record. No transformation or interpretation required.
Boundary Rationale: Pure data retrieval — no judgement involved. If the API call succeeds, you have the data. If it fails, you have an error to handle. No ambiguity, no interpretation, no risk of the agent making a wrong call.
Notes: Error path: If API unavailable, flag gap in report and continue with other sources. If account ID not found, halt and alert operator.

Action: Export all support tickets for the account within the quarterly date range. Retrieve ticket list including status, severity, resolution time, and CSAT scores.
Input: Account ID, date range (quarter start and end dates)
Output: Ticket list with status, severity, resolution time, CSAT scores per ticket
Decision Logic Detail: None — data retrieval. Filter tickets by account ID and date range. No transformation, no interpretation — the system returns a complete list or an empty result.
Boundary Rationale: Pure data retrieval with deterministic query parameters (account ID + date range). No judgement required — the API returns a complete ticket list or an error.
Notes: Error path: If API unavailable, flag gap in report and continue with other sources. If empty result, verify date range is correct and note "no tickets filed this quarter" — do not infer anything from absence of tickets.

Action: Access the product analytics platform API or data warehouse. Query for account-level usage statistics within the quarterly date range including active users, feature adoption rates, API call volume, and error rates.
Input: Account ID, date range (quarter start and end dates)
Output: Usage stats: active users, DAU/MAU ratio, feature adoption percentage, API call volume, error rates
Decision Logic Detail: None — data retrieval. Query usage API or data warehouse for account-level metrics within the date range. May require multiple API calls if metrics are spread across endpoints.
Boundary Rationale: Pure data retrieval. Usage metrics are numerical and unambiguous — the system returns them or it doesn't. No interpretation required at this stage.
Notes: Error path: If API or data warehouse unavailable, flag gap in report and continue. May need custom extraction logic depending on data warehouse setup. Pagination may be required for high-volume accounts.

Action: Search Slack channels for mentions of the account name and key contact names. Retrieve all matching message threads from the quarter. Cast a wide net — over-retrieve rather than miss relevant context.
Input: Account name, key contact names (from Step 1 output)
Output: Relevant Slack message threads mentioning the account or its contacts
Decision Logic Detail: None — search and retrieve. Use account name and key contact names as search queries against Slack API search.messages endpoint. No filtering or interpretation at this stage — retrieval only.
Boundary Rationale: Pure search and retrieval. The agent uses account name and contact names as search terms. Results will contain noise, but filtering and interpretation happen in the next step (Step 5). No judgement applied here.
Notes: Error path: If Slack API rate-limited, implement pagination with backoff and retry. If no results found, note "no Slack activity for this account this quarter" rather than omitting. Over-retrieve to ensure coverage — the human filters in Step 5.

Step Name: Review Slack context for sentiment signals
Action: Read through all retrieved Slack threads. Assess the overall tone of customer communications. Identify explicit complaints, escalation language, positive signals, and unresolved concerns. Produce an annotated sentiment summary with supporting quotes.
Output: Annotated sentiment summary: overall tone (positive / neutral / negative / mixed), key quotes, unresolved concerns, sentiment trend direction
Decision Logic Detail: Read threads and assess tone — is the customer happy, frustrated, or neutral? Look for explicit complaints ("this is broken", "unacceptable"), escalation language ("we need to talk", "escalating this"), positive signals ("love the new feature", "great support response"), and unresolved concerns that received no response. Consider thread context: who is speaking (champion vs. detractor vs. end-user), whether concerns received timely responses, recency of sentiment shifts (last 30 days weighted more heavily), and volume of communication (silence can signal disengagement). The agent summarises threads and proposes a sentiment reading; the human validates using relationship context the agent cannot access.
Boundary Rationale: Slack conversations contain sarcasm, inside jokes, off-topic tangents, and genuine product complaints mixed together. An LLM can summarise threads and propose a sentiment reading, but the CSM knows the relationship context that determines whether "we need to talk about the roadmap" is a routine quarterly planning request or a veiled threat to churn. The agent does the heavy lifting (summarise, propose); the human validates.
Notes: Error path: If no relevant threads found, mark sentiment as "insufficient data" rather than guessing neutral. If threads are ambiguous or contradictory, present both interpretations and flag for human resolution. The agent should surface direct quotes to support its proposed sentiment reading.

Action: Analyse ticket volume trends, severity distribution, resolution times vs SLA targets, and CSAT scores. Compare this quarter against the prior quarter. Produce a ticket health assessment with a clear improving/stable/degrading rating and supporting evidence.
Input: Ticket data from Step 2, SLA benchmark targets (from config)
Output: Ticket health assessment: rating (improving / stable / degrading), SLA compliance percentage, CSAT trend, key issues list, risk escalation flags
Decision Logic Detail: Evaluate first response time and resolution time against SLA targets by severity — P1: 30min response / 4hr resolution, P2: 2hr / 24hr, P3: 8hr / 72hr, P4: 24hr / 168hr. Flag if SLA compliance drops below 95% of tickets resolved within target. Flag if CSAT falls below 4.0/5. Compare ticket volume and average severity against prior quarter: a >20% volume increase or upward severity shift (e.g. average moving from Low to Medium/High) indicates degradation. If both volume and severity worsen simultaneously, escalate to the risk register. Classify overall ticket health as: Improving (volume down or SLA compliance up QoQ), Stable (all metrics within 10% of prior quarter), or Degrading (any flag triggered).
Boundary Rationale: Quantitative calculations (trend analysis, SLA compliance rates, CSAT averages) are deterministic and fully automatable — two agents given the same data would produce identical results. But interpretation of edge cases requires context the agent does not have. Example: a spike in ticket volume might look alarming, but the CSM knows it was caused by a planned migration and is therefore benign. The split boundary lets the agent handle the math while the human handles the meaning.
Systems Touched: Zendesk (output from Step 2), SLA benchmarks config file
Notes: Error path: If SLA benchmarks config is missing, use industry-standard defaults and flag for review. If ticket data is incomplete (missing severity or resolution time fields), calculate metrics on available data and note gaps. Decision logic entries here become config/scoring_rubric.yaml in Stage 5.

Action: Analyse usage trends across three core metrics: DAU/MAU ratio, feature adoption percentage, and API call volume. Compare each metric against tier-specific benchmarks and against the prior quarter. Produce an engagement assessment with a clear healthy/at-risk/critical rating.
Input: Usage data from Step 3, tier-specific benchmark thresholds (from config)
Output: Engagement assessment: rating (healthy / at-risk / critical), per-metric scores vs benchmarks, trend direction (improving / stable / declining), flagged degradation signals
Decision Logic Detail: Evaluate three metrics against tier-specific benchmarks — DAU/MAU ratio (enterprise: ≥40%, mid-market: ≥30%, SMB: ≥25%), feature adoption percentage (enterprise: ≥60%, mid-market: ≥45%, SMB: ≥35%), and API call volume floor (enterprise: 10K/month, mid-market: 2K, SMB: 500). A quarter-over-quarter decline of >15% in any single metric flags a degrading trend. Classify overall engagement as: Healthy (all metrics at or above tier benchmark with stable or improving trend), At-risk (any metric 10–25% below tier benchmark or declining for 2+ consecutive months), Critical (any metric >25% below benchmark or >15% QoQ decline). If account tier is missing from config, flag and request manual classification before proceeding.
Boundary Rationale: Tier-specific benchmark comparisons and trend calculations are deterministic — the math produces the same result every time. But usage context matters: a drop in DAU/MAU during a customer's holiday season or a known company-wide event is expected, not alarming. A sudden spike in API errors might indicate a product issue, not a customer engagement problem. The human provides the contextual interpretation that numbers alone cannot convey.
Systems Touched: Mixpanel (output from Step 3), tier benchmarks config file
Notes: Error path: If benchmark config is missing for the account's tier, flag and request manual input before scoring. If usage data is partial (e.g. DAU/MAU available but feature adoption missing), score available metrics and note gaps. These tier-specific criteria become externalised config in Stage 5.

Action: Synthesise findings from Steps 5-7 with the contract timeline from Step 1. Cross-reference sentiment, ticket health, and engagement signals to identify compound risk patterns and expansion opportunities. Produce a prioritised risk register and opportunity list.
Input: All prior step outputs (sentiment summary, ticket health assessment, engagement assessment), contract renewal date and ARR from Step 1
Output: Prioritised risk register (risk description, severity, evidence, recommended action) + opportunity list (opportunity description, evidence, recommended next step)
Decision Logic Detail: Synthesise findings across all dimensions and match against known risk/opportunity patterns. Key patterns: declining usage + upcoming renewal within 90 days = high churn risk; high feature adoption + usage above tier ceiling = upsell opportunity; negative sentiment + open P1/P2 tickets = escalation risk requiring immediate attention; positive sentiment + feature requests in Slack = expansion signal. Weigh recency of signals — a sentiment shift in the last 30 days matters more than one from 60+ days ago. Cross-reference risk signals against account tier and ARR to prioritise by business impact (high-ARR accounts with red signals get top priority). The agent proposes a risk register and opportunity list; the human validates against relationship context and knowledge of ongoing conversations not captured in data.
Boundary Rationale: Risk and opportunity identification requires synthesising multiple qualitative and quantitative signals and applying relationship context that exists only in the CSM's head. The agent can flag patterns (declining usage + upcoming renewal) and propose a risk register, but the CSM knows whether a flagged risk is already being actively managed, whether an opportunity is politically viable given the customer's budget cycle, or whether a "negative" signal is actually a positive sign of deepening engagement.
Systems Touched: All prior step outputs, Salesforce (contract renewal dates, ARR)
Notes: Error path: If one or more input assessments are missing (e.g. Slack sentiment unavailable due to API failure), generate a partial risk register and flag gaps explicitly — do not silently omit dimensions. If contract data is missing, flag that renewal timeline cannot be factored into risk assessment.

Action: Rate the account on the health framework using the Green/Amber/Red scoring rubric. Evaluate each of the four dimensions (tickets, usage, sentiment, contract) independently, then assign an overall score based on the most severe dimension. Provide a written justification referencing the specific criteria that drove the rating.
Input: All prior step outputs (ticket health assessment from Step 6; engagement assessment from Step 7; sentiment summary from Step 5; contract data from Step 1; risk register from Step 8)
Output: Overall health score (Green / Amber / Red) with per-dimension sub-scores and a written justification paragraph
Decision Logic Detail: Apply Green/Amber/Red rating across four dimensions — tickets, usage, sentiment, and contract. Green: SLA compliance ≥95%, no open P1s, CSAT ≥4.0; DAU/MAU and feature adoption at or above tier benchmark with stable or improving trend; neutral-to-positive sentiment with no unresolved escalations; no renewal within 90 days, or renewal approaching with no risk signals. Amber: SLA compliance 80–95%, or unresolved P2+ tickets open >7 days, or CSAT 3.5–4.0; metrics 10–25% below tier benchmark or declining for 2+ consecutive months; mixed sentiment with some concerns; renewal within 90 days with one or more amber signals in other dimensions. Red: SLA compliance <80%, or any open P1, or CSAT <3.5; any metric >25% below benchmark or >15% QoQ decline; negative tone or active escalation or executive complaint; renewal within 60 days with any red signal in another dimension. Assign the overall score as the most severe rating triggered by any single dimension. When dimensions conflict (e.g. green tickets but red sentiment), weight sentiment and contract risk more heavily — dissatisfied customers with upcoming renewals are the highest priority regardless of other metrics.
Boundary Rationale: The scoring rubric itself is criteria-based and the quantitative scoring is automatable, but the final health score assignment is HIL because these scores drive account strategy decisions with significant business consequences — resource allocation, executive attention, renewal negotiation approach. The human validates that the rubric-derived score matches their holistic understanding of the account, catching cases where the rubric produces a technically correct but contextually misleading result (e.g. a Green score for an account the CSM knows is quietly evaluating competitors).
Systems Touched: All prior step outputs, health framework criteria config
Notes: Error path: If any dimension cannot be scored due to missing data, assign that dimension as "Unable to score — insufficient data" and note the gap in the justification. Do not default missing dimensions to Green. These criteria become the core of config/scoring_rubric.yaml in Stage 5.

Action: Write a structured report using the standard template with sections for each dimension: ticket health, product engagement, sentiment analysis, risk register, opportunity list, and overall health score. Each section includes metric summary, trend analysis, key findings, and recommended actions. Populate all sections from prior step outputs.
Input: All prior step outputs (Steps 1-9), report template
Output: Formatted health report document (Markdown or HTML) with all sections populated, per-dimension detail, and recommended actions
Decision Logic Detail: None — template-based generation. Populate the standard report template with data and assessments from prior steps. Each section follows a fixed structure: metric summary, trend vs prior quarter, key findings, recommended actions. No new analysis or judgement — this step assembles and formats existing outputs into a coherent narrative document.
Boundary Rationale: Report structure is template-based and all content has been validated in prior HIL steps. The agent generates a complete draft; the human reviews for narrative coherence, tone appropriateness, and any sensitive content that needs rewording or emphasis adjustment before it goes to the exec summary step.
Systems Touched: All prior step outputs, report template
Notes: Error path: If report template is missing or malformed, generate a plain-text structured report with standard section headers and flag for manual formatting. If any prior step output is missing, include the section with a placeholder noting "data unavailable" rather than omitting the section entirely. Output as Markdown or HTML.

Action: Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary distilling the full health report into the key message. Lead with overall health status, follow with the single most important risk or opportunity, close with the recommended next action. Calibrate tone to the executive audience.
Input: Health report from Step 10, overall health score from Step 9
Output: Executive summary paragraph (3-5 sentences): overall status, top risk or opportunity, recommended action
Decision Logic Detail: Distill the full health report into a 3-5 sentence executive summary. Lead with overall health status (Green/Amber/Red and what it means in plain language). Follow with the single most important risk or opportunity — the one thing the reader must know. Close with the recommended next action or ask. Calibrate tone to the audience: executives want the "so what" and the ask, not the methodology or the data. If the account is Red, lead with the risk and the urgency of the ask. If Green, lead with the opportunity and momentum. If Amber, lead with what is changing and what decision is needed. Avoid hedging language ("might", "could potentially") — be direct about what the data shows and what you recommend.
Boundary Rationale: The agent can draft a competent summary from structured report data, but executive communication requires tone calibration and political sensitivity that the agent cannot evaluate. The human adjusts emphasis (what to highlight vs. downplay), manages framing for the specific executive audience, and ensures the summary will land correctly with the reader. A tone-deaf exec summary undermines trust in the entire report.
Notes: Error path: If health report from Step 10 is incomplete, generate summary from available sections and explicitly flag gaps ("Note: sentiment data unavailable for this quarter"). The tone review is critical — an AI-generated summary that reads as generic or tone-deaf to the executive audience undermines trust in the entire workflow.

Action: Review the complete report package (health report + exec summary) for accuracy, completeness, tone, and political sensitivity. Verify that narrative sections are consistent with scored dimensions. Check that sensitive account details are appropriately framed. Select the distribution list (varies by account based on stakeholder preferences and org structure). Make final edits to wording, emphasis, and framing. Send to stakeholders via email or internal communication platform.
Input: Complete report package (health report from Step 10, exec summary from Step 11), stakeholder contact list
Output: Distributed report package sent to appropriate stakeholders
Decision Logic Detail: Review the complete package for: (1) Accuracy — do narrative sections match the scored dimensions? (2) Completeness — are all sections present and populated? (3) Tone — is the language appropriate for the audience and the account situation? (4) Political sensitivity — is there anything that needs rewording or omitting given current account dynamics? (5) Distribution list — who should receive this report? The list may vary by account based on stakeholder preferences, org changes, or current account dynamics (e.g. adding a new VP who just joined, removing someone who left). Make final edits and send.
Boundary Rationale: Distribution is irreversible — once the report is sent, it is sent. The CSM needs to make final judgement calls about tone, completeness, and political sensitivity. The distribution list requires relationship knowledge the agent cannot access (who is in, who is out, who needs a heads-up before the formal report). This is a deliberate safety boundary — keeping this step fully manual is not a limitation, it is a design choice.
Systems Touched: Email / communication platform, complete report package
Notes: Error path: N/A — this is a fully manual step. The human is the error handler. Do not attempt to automate distribution in v1. This is the strongest safety boundary in the workflow. The boundary can be reconsidered in future versions as trust builds.
